And apparently, the endorsement of President Barack Obama, obviously not enough to overcome that.
Exactly.
It never felt that this race was about President Obama up there.
It felt like it was more about Arlen Specter and the party switch.
But the Obama endorsement sure...
Did it feel like in the last few days of the race, the White House was peddling away from Arlen Specter?
They could see where the polls were.
They knew where they might have to be today, the day after.
So they definitely were prepared for the worst and backing off a bit.
Ken?
And one thing we have to remember is we oversell the importance of presidential endorsements, especially when the president himself is not on the ballot.
We saw that with Ronald Reagan in 1986, when he tried to save the Republican control of the Senate.
We saw that with President Obama in New Jersey, in Virginia, in Massachusetts with Martha Coakley.
I think it's overrated.
Of course, the president is popular with a lot of rank and file, but whether he can change minds, change the vote with candidates who when he's not on the ballot himself remains a question.
In this case, it seemed to be just more reassuring.
It was President Obama saying folks, yes, he's a real Democrat.
I know he was a Republican forever.
But again, Sestak used it really effectively against him, the party switch.
